title: Low-FODMAP Macro Calculator | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Low-FODMAP Macros Calculator for IBS & SIBO
description: Low-FODMAP macros: 20% protein, 30% fat, 50% low-FODMAP carbs. For IBS and SIBO with 70% symptom improvement. Monash protocol.
intro: The **low-FODMAP diet** (Monash University, 2005) reduces short-chain carbohydrates that ferment in the intestine: **F**ermentable **O**ligosaccharides, **D**isaccharides, **M**onosaccharides **A**nd **P**olyols. Recommended for irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) and small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO) with evidence of symptom improvement in 70% of patients. Standard distribution is **20% protein / 30% fat / 50% carbohydrates**, but carbs are chosen from low-FODMAP sources. It follows 3 phases: **1) Elimination (2–6 weeks)**; **2) Gradual reintroduction (6–8 weeks)**; **3) Personalization**. This calculator provides your daily macros plus allowed and restricted foods by FODMAP category.
keyTakeaway: Low-FODMAP: **20% protein + 30% fat + 50% low-FODMAP carbs**. 3 phases. Improves 70% of IBS cases.
useCases: Your gastroenterologist or dietitian recommended it.

example.title: Example: 2000 kcal Low-FODMAP
steps: **Low-FODMAP carbs**: **250 g** from rice, potato, carrot.
example.result: Typical day: chicken + rice + carrot + strawberry + olive oil. Avoid onion, garlic, wheat, dairy.

explanation:
## What is Low-FODMAP?

Reduces fermentable carbs that trigger symptoms.

## 5 FODMAP Groups

- **Fructans** (wheat, onion, garlic).
- **GOS** (legumes, broccoli).
- **Lactose**.
- **Excess fructose** (apple, mango, honey).
- **Polyols** (pear, sorbitol, xylitol).

## Allowed (Low-FODMAP)

- Rice, potato, polenta, quinoa, oats.
- Chicken, beef, fish, eggs.
- Tomato, cucumber, carrot, spinach.
- Strawberry, ripe banana, kiwi, orange.
- Lactose-free options, aged cheeses.
- Olive oil, avocado.

## Foods to Avoid

- Wheat, onion, garlic.
- Legumes.
- Lactose-containing foods.
- Apple, pear, mango, watermelon.
- Honey, sorbitol, xylitol.

## 3 Phases

### Phase 1 – Elimination (2–6 weeks)
Remove all high-FODMAP foods.

### Phase 2 – Reintroduction (6–8 weeks)
Gradually reintroduce foods one at a time.

### Phase 3 – Personalization
Avoid only foods that trigger your symptoms.

## Evidence

- **70% symptom improvement in IBS** (2017 meta-analysis).

## Related Tools

- [Histamine in Food](/calculadora-histamina-alimento-intolerancia).
- [Fructose Malabsorption](/calculadora-fructosa-malabsorcion).
- [Lactose in Food](/calculadora-lactosa-alimento-intolerancia).

**This is not a maintenance diet**. Consult a registered dietitian before starting.

faq.q: How long does the elimination phase last?
faq.a: 2–6 weeks, depending on symptom severity and tolerance.
No, but it improves symptoms in approximately 70% of people with IBS.
Yes, it works best when combined with antibiotics or herbal antimicrobials prescribed by your doctor.
No. It's a temporary elimination diet. Phase 2 gradually reintroduces foods to identify your specific triggers.
It's challenging but possible. Focus on firm tofu, tempeh, and low-FODMAP legumes like lentils in small portions.
The Monash FODMAP app (official from Monash University) is the gold standard for checking foods.

faq.q: What foods can I eat on day one?
faq.a: Chicken, rice, carrot, strawberry, banana, spinach, olive oil. Avoid onion, garlic, wheat, and dairy.
Yes. Work with a registered dietitian familiar with low-FODMAP to avoid nutritional deficiencies and ensure proper progression through phases.

sources.name: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health
